title: Fitting skirting board: external corners
subtitle: Mitre both pieces, glue the joint, pin from behind
excerpt: How to cut and fit skirting board around an external corner using a mitre joint: measuring and marking the 45-degree mitres, cutting accurately, gluing and pinning the joint, and filling before painting.
sourceNotes: Standard UK joinery practice. Newey and Drage Practical Carpentry and Joinery. City & Guilds Site Carpentry Level 2 syllabus.

recipeTools.notes: Adequate for a single external corner.
Faster for a full room's skirting.
For filling the joint before painting.

content.text: An external corner on skirting is cut as a mitre: both pieces are cut at 45 degrees so the two face profiles meet at a sharp point at the corner. Unlike the internal corner cope, which accommodates movement, a mitre joint at an external corner is a fixed joint that relies on glue, a pin, and a slight back-cut to close tight at the face.
Measuring
Step 1, measure to the corner point.

Measure each skirting length to the outside of the corner, not to the wall face. The outside point is the longest point of the mitre. Mark this measurement on the top face of the skirting board; the mitre cut will run from this mark back to the shorter back face dimension.
Cutting the mitres
Step 2, set the saw to 45 degrees and cut.

Set the mitre saw to 45 degrees external mitre. The face of the board is the longest dimension; the cut angles away from the face to the back. Cut the first piece, then the second. The two cut faces should be mirror images that close together at the corner point.
Step 3, back-cut if needed.

If the corner is not exactly 90 degrees (and most are not), a square 45-degree mitre will not close tight at the face. Check the dry fit first. If the face is open, increase the mitre angle slightly beyond 45 degrees on both pieces (to 46 or 47 degrees) so the face edges meet while the back faces remain clear.
Fitting
Step 4, fix the first piece.

Fix the first skirting run to the wall up to the corner, leaving the mitre face dry (no adhesive on the mitre yet). The last fixing should be at least 150 mm before the corner so the mitre end can move slightly when the second piece is pressed home.
Step 5, glue and close the mitre.

Apply PVA wood glue to both mitre faces. Press the second piece onto the first at the corner and nail through the second piece face into the first using the pin hammer and a 40 mm oval nail or lost-head nail angled into the corner. This locks the joint closed while the glue sets.
Step 6, fill and paint.
Once the glue has cured, fill any small gap at the mitre face with a fine filler and sand flush. Prime any bare wood at the mitre and apply the finish coat.
Common mistakes
Mitre opening at the face after the glue dries.

The corner was not exactly 90 degrees and the joint was not adjusted. Fill the gap with a flexible decorator's filler that can take paint without cracking; a stiff filler will crack as the timber moves.
Mitre face chipped or torn.
The saw blade was dull or the cut was made too fast. A fine-tooth blade at a slow feed rate gives a clean mitre cut in MDF skirting. Re-cut with a sharp blade.
